summary: My name is Masayo Matsumoto, born in 1893 in Nagano Prefecture during the 26th year of Meiji. I was raised by my father, who was a Shinto priest and a public office employee, and my mother, who was very strict and religious. Despite many health challenges, I graduated from teacher's college in the Taishō period and later became a Christian, which caused tension in my family. I moved to the United States and have reflected often on my upbringing and faith.

I was born and raised in Nagano Prefecture, a place where my family had deep roots and connections. My father was involved in village politics there, and we lived near Mt. Ontake and the Kiso River. It was in Nagano that I experienced my early education, family struggles, and religious upbringing, including visits to local shrines like Suwa Shrine. Later, I moved away but always remembered my home prefecture fondly.

I was born in 1893, which was the 26th year of the Meiji period, a time of great change in Japan. My father studied in Tokyo during this era, even though it was against his own father's wishes. The Meiji period shaped much of my family's history and values, including the influence of Shinto and the modernization of Japan that affected our lives.

My family was deeply connected to Shinto; my father was part of the Shinto priesthood for years, and our household observed many Shinto customs. My mother made us pray before the Shinto altar daily, and we were taught to respect the gods at shrines like Suwa Shrine. However, my father struggled with his belief in Shinto gods, seeing them as human creations, which caused some tension in our religious life.

My father went down to Tokyo in his twenties to study, despite his father's opposition. While there, he worked and studied simultaneously and met people like Hakubun Ito and a rickshaw driver from his village. Tokyo represented a place of opportunity and struggle for my father during the Meiji period, shaping our family story.

Hakubun Ito was someone my father and his fellow working students sought out for work when they were in Tokyo. My father and others went to his house looking for employment, which was part of their efforts to support themselves while studying. This connection was part of my father's early struggles in the city.

The Qbon festival was an event where the rickshaw driver's daughter, who later became my mother, sang beautifully. This festival played a role in my parents' meeting and eventual marriage, despite opposition due to her tenant farmer background. The festival holds a special place in my family history.

When I became very sick with rheumatism, pleurisy, and heart issues, my father sold property to send me to Nagoya University Hospital for six months of treatment. This hospital was crucial in my recovery and allowed me to return to teacher's college despite my illness. It was a difficult but important time in my life.

I graduated from teacher's college in the fourth year of the Taishō period, a time when Japan was undergoing social and political changes. This era was significant in my education and early career, as well as in my family's struggles with religion and modernity. The Taishō period shaped much of my adult life.

My father became interested in the Tenri sect during a time when he was questioning Shinto beliefs. This interest came as he struggled with his faith and considered moving to Nagoya with me. Tenrikyo represented an alternative religious path during a period of spiritual searching in our family.

My mother made us bow whenever we passed Suwa Shrine at the foot of Mt. Ontake, emphasizing respect for the gods. I also prayed there, sacrificing my favorite food in hopes of my mother's recovery. Suwa Shrine was a significant spiritual place in my childhood and family tradition.

Mt. Ontake was near our home, and my mother instructed us to bow at shrines located at its foot. It was part of our daily religious practice and a symbol of the spiritual environment in which I was raised. The mountain and its shrines were intertwined with my family's Shinto beliefs.

I read verses from the Gospel of Matthew, chapter 34, which initially confused me but later became meaningful as I explored Christianity. The Bible was given to me by church members and was a source of spiritual guidance during my conversion. Reading Matthew was part of my journey away from Shinto and toward Christian faith.

Reverend Kanzo Ogawa was the assistant minister at the church I attended, and he was a follower of Reverend Kagawa. He and his mother were very kind to me, and his mother came from an aristocratic family connected to the Byakkotai and Tokugawa clan. Kanzo Ogawa played an important role in my early Christian experience.

Reverend Kagawa Kanzo was a prominent Christian leader whose follower, Reverend Kanzo Ogawa, assisted at the church I attended. Reverend Kagawa was fond of Ogawa throughout his life, and his influence extended to many in Nagano and beyond. Though I did not meet him directly, his work impacted my spiritual journey.

Reverend Hiraiwa of the Methodist Church came to Nagano Prefecture as a superintendent and gave a sermon that deeply moved me. His words helped me resolve feelings of guilt about becoming a Christian against my parents' wishes. The Methodist Church was the community where I found spiritual support and fellowship.

Nagano Prefecture is where I was born and raised, surrounded by mountains like Mt. Ontake and rivers such as the Kiso River. It was here that my family lived, my father served as village chief, and I attended school. Nagano shaped much of my early life, traditions, and religious upbringing.

Reverend Hiraiwa was a superintendent from the Methodist Church who came to Nagano and gave a sermon that inspired me to become a Christian. His message about overcoming guilt and making my parents happy after baptism was very meaningful. His influence helped me make a firm decision about my faith.

The Kiso River was near my home in Nagano Prefecture, where my father and a neighboring village chief built a bridge. It was part of the community infrastructure and symbolized my father's role as a village leader. Crossing the river was part of my daily life and memories.

Reverend Kunihara from Nagoya Central Church baptized me after I expressed my desire to become a Christian. Despite opposition from my family and church ministers, I was determined to follow my faith, and Reverend Kunihara supported me in this important step. His role was pivotal in my spiritual transformation.

Nagoya Central Church was where I was baptized by Reverend Kunihara. Although it was some distance from my home, I visited regularly and participated in prayer meetings and Sunday school teaching during vacations. The church community provided me with spiritual support and fellowship.

The mother of Reverend Kanzo Ogawa was from an aristocratic family that had ties to the Byakkotai, a group known in our history. She was kind and gracious, often preparing meals for me when I visited. The connection to the Byakkotai added a sense of historical depth to my church experience.

Reverend Kanzo Ogawa's mother was related to the Tokugawa clan through her aristocratic lineage. This connection was notable in our community and added prestige to the church family I was involved with. It highlighted the blend of traditional and modern influences in my life.

I saw some troubling things among ministers in Los Angeles, which made me cautious about encouraging my sister to become a Christian. Although I was positive about my own faith, my experiences in Los Angeles influenced my protective attitude toward my family. The city represented a place of both opportunity and concern for me.

Zen Buddhism was part of the religious landscape around me, but my family was primarily Shinto, and later I embraced Christianity. Although Zen Buddhism was not my personal faith, it was one of the spiritual traditions present in Japan during my upbringing. It contrasted with the beliefs I eventually adopted.

Though I lived in the United States later in life, the President of the United States was a distant figure in my story, representing the country where I sought new opportunities. My focus was more on my personal and family journey rather than political leaders. However, living in the U.S. shaped my experiences significantly.

During my schooling, I was honored to guard pictures of the Emperor and Empress, a duty I took seriously as a way to serve my country. The Emperor represented the nation and its traditions, which I respected even as I explored new religious beliefs. This role was a source of pride despite my frequent absences due to illness.

entity: Coronation of the Emperor of Japan
summary: I was involved in school activities during the time of the Emperor's coronation, which was a significant national event. Guarding the imperial pictures was part of the preparations and responsibilities we had. This event connected me to Japan's history and cultural heritage.

Although I did not attend Aoyama University myself, it was part of the broader educational context in Japan during my time. My own education was at a teacher's college, but institutions like Aoyama represented the expanding opportunities for learning in the Taishō period. Education was highly valued in my family.

Manchuria was a place mentioned in the broader context of Japan's history during my lifetime, but I did not have a direct personal connection to it. It was part of the geopolitical events that influenced Japan and its people. My focus remained on my family and faith journey.

Yamanashi Prefecture was near Nagano, but I did not live there myself. It was part of the regional landscape of central Japan that shaped the culture and environment of my upbringing. My experiences were centered more in Nagano and Gifu Prefectures.

Azabu Junior High School was not part of my personal education, but it represents the kind of schooling available in Japan during my era. My own schooling was in Nagano and teacher's college. Education was strict and demanding, as I experienced firsthand.

I eventually came to California, living in places like Tule Lake and Pasadena, where I was interviewed. California was where I sought new opportunities and lived as an immigrant from Japan. It was a significant part of my later life and experiences.

Tule Lake, California, was one of the places where I lived in the United States. It was part of my immigrant experience, and I recall it as a location connected to my family's life abroad. Tule Lake was a chapter in my journey far from Japan.

Heihachiro Takarabe was the interviewer who recorded my oral history in Pasadena in 1973. His questions helped me reflect on my life story, from my birth in Nagano to my experiences in the United States. I am grateful for his role in preserving my memories.
